title: What is Machine Learning?
subject: Machine Learning
content: Machine learning is a field that focuses on learning useful patterns from data. Instead of writing fixed rules manually, a machine learning algorithm uses examples and features to build a model that can support prediction, classification, grouping, or data understanding. Machine learning is used in many practical situations such as predicting customer churn, estimating house prices, detecting risk, segmenting customers, and identifying useful patterns in large data sets. The type of machine learning method depends on the learning task and whether the data contains a known target value.

title: Supervised Learning
subject: Machine Learning
content: Supervised learning is used when the training data includes a target value that the model is expected to predict. The model learns from input variables, also called features or predictors, and tries to estimate an output variable, also called the target, response, or outcome. The target value supervises the learning process because it shows the correct answer during training. Supervised learning is commonly used for tasks such as predicting house prices, customer churn, employee attrition, patient readmission risk, and production time.

title: Regression Problems
subject: Machine Learning
content: Regression is a supervised learning problem where the goal is to predict a numeric outcome. The predicted value usually falls on a continuous range rather than belonging to a fixed category. Examples include predicting a house sale price, estimating time to market, or forecasting a numerical quantity. In regression, the model studies the relationship between predictor variables and a continuous response value. Regression is a problem type, while linear regression is only one specific method that can be used to solve regression problems.

title: Classification Problems
subject: Machine Learning
content: Classification is a supervised learning problem where the goal is to predict a categorical outcome. The output may be binary, such as yes or no, or it may include multiple classes. Examples include predicting whether a customer will churn, whether a coupon will be redeemed, whether an online ad will be clicked, or whether a review is positive or negative. Classification models often calculate probabilities for each class, then choose the class with the highest predicted probability as the final prediction.

title: Unsupervised Learning
subject: Machine Learning
content: Unsupervised learning is used when the data does not contain a target variable. The goal is not to predict a known answer, but to discover hidden structure, patterns, or groups in the data. Common unsupervised learning tasks include clustering and dimension reduction. Clustering groups similar observations together, while dimension reduction reduces the number of variables while keeping important information. Unsupervised learning is often used during exploratory data analysis, but evaluating its result can be more subjective because there is no known correct target value.

title: Data Preparation for Machine Learning
subject: Machine Learning
content: Before applying machine learning, the data usually needs to be prepared. Data preparation may include selecting useful features, removing unnecessary variables, renaming variables to make them meaningful, recoding values, and handling missing data. Clean and well-prepared data helps the model learn more reliable patterns. The quality of the data preparation stage strongly affects the quality of the final machine learning model because models depend directly on the information provided in the training data.

title: Machine Learning Modeling Process
subject: Machine Learning
content: The modeling process describes the steps used to build, test, and improve a machine learning model. It usually begins by understanding the problem and preparing the data, then splitting the data into training and testing parts. The model is trained on one part of the data and evaluated on data it has not seen before. This process helps estimate how well the model may perform on future observations. A good modeling process also includes validation, tuning, and performance measurement.

title: Data Splitting
subject: Machine Learning
content: Data splitting is the process of dividing a data set into separate parts for training and evaluation. The training set is used to fit the model, while the testing set is used to estimate how well the model performs on unseen data. Data splitting helps prevent the model from being evaluated only on examples it has already learned from. Common approaches include simple random sampling and stratified sampling. Stratified sampling is useful when the target classes are imbalanced because it helps preserve class proportions across the splits.

title: Resampling Methods
subject: Machine Learning
content: Resampling methods are used to estimate model performance more reliably by repeatedly training and evaluating models on different parts of the data. A common method is k-fold cross validation, where the data is divided into k parts and the model is trained multiple times, each time using a different part for validation. Another method is bootstrapping, which creates repeated samples from the original data. Resampling is important because one single train-test split may give an unstable or misleading estimate of model performance.

title: Bias-Variance Tradeoff
subject: Machine Learning
content: The bias-variance tradeoff explains two common sources of model error. Bias occurs when a model is too simple and cannot capture the real pattern in the data, which may lead to underfitting. Variance occurs when a model is too sensitive to the training data and captures noise instead of general patterns, which may lead to overfitting. A good model should balance bias and variance so that it performs well not only on the training data but also on new unseen data.

title: Model Evaluation
subject: Machine Learning
content: Model evaluation measures how well a machine learning model performs. The evaluation method depends on the type of problem. For regression models, common evaluation measures compare predicted numeric values with actual numeric values. For classification models, evaluation measures focus on whether the predicted class matches the actual class and may also consider class probabilities. Evaluation is essential because a model that performs well on training data may still fail on new data if it has overfit the training examples.

title: Feature Engineering
subject: Machine Learning
content: Feature engineering is the process of preparing and transforming input variables so that machine learning models can learn from them more effectively. It may include handling missing values, transforming numeric variables, encoding categorical variables, reducing skewness, standardizing values, and creating new features from existing ones. Good feature engineering can improve model performance because many algorithms depend heavily on how the data is represented. Poorly prepared features may make it difficult for the model to identify useful patterns.

title: Linear Regression
subject: Machine Learning
content: Linear regression is a supervised learning method used for regression problems. It models the relationship between one or more input variables and a numeric output by fitting a linear equation to the data. Simple linear regression uses one predictor, while multiple linear regression uses several predictors. The model estimates coefficients that describe how changes in the input variables are associated with changes in the target value. Linear regression is often used as an interpretable baseline model because its structure is relatively easy to understand.

title: Logistic Regression
subject: Machine Learning
content: Logistic regression is a supervised learning method commonly used for classification problems. Instead of predicting a continuous numeric value, it estimates the probability that an observation belongs to a particular class. It is especially common in binary classification, where the outcome has two possible values such as yes or no. The model can use one or more predictors to estimate class probability. Logistic regression is useful because it provides both predictions and interpretable information about how features influence the probability of an outcome.

title: Regularized Regression
subject: Machine Learning
content: Regularized regression extends ordinary regression by adding a penalty that controls model complexity. This helps reduce overfitting, especially when the data contains many predictors or correlated variables. Common forms include ridge regression, lasso regression, and elastic net. Ridge regression shrinks coefficients toward smaller values, lasso can shrink some coefficients to zero, and elastic net combines both ideas. Regularization is useful when the goal is to improve prediction performance and create a more stable model.

title: Decision Trees
subject: Machine Learning
content: Decision trees are supervised learning models that make predictions by splitting the data into smaller groups based on feature values. The model has a tree-like structure, where each internal node represents a decision rule and each leaf represents a prediction. Decision trees can be used for both regression and classification problems. They are easy to interpret because their decisions can be followed step by step. However, deep trees may overfit the data, so techniques such as early stopping and pruning are often used to control complexity.

title: Random Forests
subject: Machine Learning
content: Random forests are ensemble models that combine many decision trees to improve prediction performance. Each tree is trained on a different sample of the data, and randomness is added when selecting features for splits. For classification, the forest usually combines tree votes to select the final class. For regression, it averages the predictions of the trees. Random forests often perform well without heavy tuning because combining many trees reduces the instability of a single decision tree and improves generalization.

title: Gradient Boosting
subject: Machine Learning
content: Gradient boosting is an ensemble learning method that builds models sequentially. Each new model tries to correct the errors made by the previous models. Instead of training many independent trees, boosting focuses on gradually improving performance by learning from residual errors. Gradient boosting can produce highly accurate models for both regression and classification tasks, but it usually requires careful tuning of hyperparameters such as learning rate, number of trees, tree depth, and sampling settings.

title: Deep Learning
subject: Machine Learning
content: Deep learning uses neural networks with multiple layers to learn complex patterns from data. A deep neural network contains input layers, hidden layers, and output layers. Each layer transforms the data and passes information to the next layer. Deep learning is useful for complex problems such as image recognition, speech processing, and large-scale pattern recognition. Training deep networks usually requires careful choices about architecture, activation functions, model capacity, regularization, learning rate, and optimization settings.

title: Interpretable Machine Learning
subject: Machine Learning
content: Interpretable machine learning focuses on understanding how models make predictions. This is especially important for complex models that may behave like black boxes. Interpretation can be global, explaining the overall behavior of a model, or local, explaining one specific prediction. Common interpretation techniques include feature importance, partial dependence, individual conditional expectation, feature interaction analysis, LIME, and Shapley values. Interpretable machine learning helps users trust, diagnose, and improve models by connecting predictions back to meaningful features.

title: Introduction to Deep Learning
subject: Deep Learning
content: Deep learning is a machine learning approach that learns data representations through multiple layers of transformation. Traditional shallow models usually use one or two transformations of the feature space, while deep neural networks use successive layers to learn more complex representations. This makes deep learning useful for high-dimensional data such as images, videos, sound, and other complex inputs. Deep learning models map features to targets using transformations and feedback signals, but their main strength is the ability to learn meaningful representations across several hidden layers.

title: Why Deep Learning?
subject: Deep Learning
content: Deep learning became important because some problems require models that can learn complex patterns from large amounts of data. Problems such as handwritten digit recognition, image classification, speech recognition, facial recognition, and autonomous driving involve many possible feature representations. In handwritten digit recognition, for example, useful information may include angles, edges, line thickness, curves, and the completeness of shapes. Deep neural networks can learn these representations automatically through hidden layers instead of requiring all features to be manually designed before modeling.

title: Neural Network Layers
subject: Deep Learning
content: A basic neural network contains three main types of layers: an input layer, one or more hidden layers, and an output layer. The input layer receives the original features from the data. The hidden layer is where most of the learning takes place because it transforms the input features into useful internal representations. The output layer produces the final prediction. In deeper networks, there are multiple hidden layers, and each layer can learn a different level of representation from the previous layer.

title: MNIST Data for Deep Learning
subject: Deep Learning
content: The MNIST data set is commonly used to explain neural networks and deep learning concepts. It contains images of handwritten digits, where the goal is to predict the correct digit from 0 to 9. Each image is represented as numeric pixel values, so it can be used as input to a neural network. MNIST is useful for teaching deep learning because it shows how a model can learn visual patterns from pixel data and classify each image into one of several possible digit classes.

title: Preparing Input Features
subject: Deep Learning
content: Feedforward deep neural networks require feature inputs to be numeric. If the data contains categorical variables, they must be converted into numeric form using methods such as one-hot encoding or integer label encoding. Deep neural networks are also sensitive to the scale of feature values, so feature standardization or scaling is important before training. For image data such as MNIST, pixel values can be divided by 255 so the values become scaled between 0 and 1. Preparing inputs correctly helps the network train more effectively.

title: Encoding Target Values
subject: Deep Learning
content: When the response variable contains multiple classes, the target values often need to be converted into a one-hot encoded matrix. For example, in MNIST the response classes are digits from 0 to 9. Instead of keeping each target as a single number, the target can be represented as a vector with ten positions, where the correct class is marked with 1 and the others are marked with 0. This format allows the neural network output layer to produce probabilities for each possible class.

title: Feedforward Neural Networks
subject: Deep Learning
content: A feedforward neural network, also called a multilayer perceptron, is a neural network where information moves in one direction from the input layer through hidden layers to the output layer. Each layer receives information from the previous layer and passes transformed information to the next layer. Feedforward networks are densely connected when each node in one layer is connected to nodes in the next layer. This architecture is the foundation for many deep learning methods and is used to introduce the main ideas behind deep neural networks.

title: Core Components of a Deep Neural Network
subject: Deep Learning
content: To build a feedforward deep neural network, four main components are needed. The first is input data, which contains the features and target values. The second is a predefined network architecture, which includes layers, nodes, and activation functions. The third is a feedback mechanism that allows the network to measure its prediction error and learn from it. The fourth is a model training approach, where the model repeatedly updates its weights to reduce the loss function and improve prediction accuracy.

title: Network Architecture
subject: Deep Learning
content: Network architecture describes how a neural network is structured. In a feedforward deep neural network, the main architectural decisions are the number of layers, the number of nodes in each layer, and the activation functions used inside the layers. Layers and nodes control the model's capacity and complexity. More layers and more nodes allow the model to learn more complex patterns, but they can also increase the risk of overfitting if the model becomes too powerful for the available data.

title: Layers and Nodes
subject: Deep Learning
content: Layers and nodes are the basic building blocks of a deep neural network. A node receives inputs, applies weights and a bias value, then passes the result through an activation function. A layer contains multiple nodes working together. Dense layers are fully connected layers where nodes in one layer are connected to nodes in the next layer. Increasing the number of layers or nodes gives the model more capacity to learn complicated patterns, but it also increases computational cost and the chance of memorizing noise.

title: Hidden Layers
subject: Deep Learning
content: Hidden layers are the internal layers between the input layer and the output layer. They are called hidden because their values are not directly observed as final predictions. Hidden layers learn intermediate representations of the data. In image recognition, early hidden layers may learn simple patterns such as edges or angles, while later hidden layers may learn more complex patterns such as shapes or object parts. The number and size of hidden layers influence how complex the model can become.

title: Output Layers
subject: Deep Learning
content: The output layer produces the final prediction of a neural network. Its design depends on the type of problem. For regression, the output layer may have one node that predicts a continuous numeric value. For binary classification, the output layer often produces a probability for one class. For multiclass classification, the output layer usually contains one node for each class. In the MNIST example, the output layer has ten nodes because there are ten possible digit classes from 0 to 9.

title: Activation Functions
subject: Deep Learning
content: Activation functions determine whether the information received by a node should be passed forward to the next layer. A node combines incoming inputs with weights and a bias value, then applies an activation function to the result. Common activation functions include linear, ReLU, sigmoid, and softmax. ReLU is often used in hidden layers because it allows positive signals to pass while setting negative values to zero. The choice of activation function depends on the layer and the type of prediction problem.

title: Output Activation Choices
subject: Deep Learning
content: The output activation function should match the prediction task. For regression problems, a linear activation function is commonly used because the output is a continuous numeric value. For binary classification, a sigmoid activation function is often used because it produces a probability between 0 and 1. For multiclass classification, a softmax activation function is commonly used because it converts output values into probabilities across several classes. In the MNIST example, softmax is used because the model must choose among ten digit classes.

title: Backpropagation
subject: Deep Learning
content: Backpropagation is the process that allows a neural network to learn from its errors. During a forward pass, the network makes predictions using the current weights. Then the model compares the predictions with the actual target values using a loss function. The network works backward through the layers to calculate how the weights contributed to the error. It then adjusts the weights in a direction that reduces the loss. Repeating this process many times helps the model improve its predictions.

title: Loss Functions and Optimizers
subject: Deep Learning
content: A loss function measures how far the model's predictions are from the correct answers. For regression, mean squared error may be used. For classification, binary cross entropy or categorical cross entropy may be used depending on the number of classes. An optimizer controls how the model updates its weights to reduce the loss. Optimizers are variants of gradient descent and differ in how they adjust the learning process. Common optimizers include RMSProp, Adam, and Adagrad.

title: Model Training
subject: Deep Learning
content: Model training is the process of fitting a neural network to data. During training, the model receives batches of observations and updates its weights using mini-batch stochastic gradient descent. Important training settings include batch size, epochs, validation split, and verbosity. Batch size controls how many observations are processed before an update. An epoch means the model has seen the entire training data once. A validation split holds out part of the data to estimate out-of-sample performance during training.

title: Model Capacity
subject: Deep Learning
content: Model capacity refers to how much complexity a neural network can learn. A model with more layers and more nodes has higher capacity and can learn more patterns from the data. However, high capacity can also lead to overfitting because the model may memorize noise in the training data instead of learning general patterns. A good deep learning model should have enough capacity to capture important relationships while still performing well on validation data.

title: Regularization and Dropout
subject: Deep Learning
content: Regularization is used to reduce overfitting in deep neural networks. Weight regularization adds a penalty to large weights, which encourages the model to rely on strong and consistent signals rather than noise. L1 and L2 penalties can be used, and L2 regularization is often called weight decay in neural networks. Dropout is another common regularization method. It randomly sets some layer outputs to zero during training, which prevents the network from depending too strongly on specific nodes and helps improve generalization.

title: Learning Rate and Grid Search
subject: Deep Learning
content: The learning rate controls how strongly the model updates its weights during training. If the learning rate is too large, the model may fail to settle near a good solution. If it is too small, training may become slow or get stuck. Learning rate strategies can adjust the rate when validation loss stops improving. Early stopping can also stop training before overfitting becomes worse. Grid search is used to compare different hyperparameter combinations such as number of nodes, dropout rates, optimizer choice, and learning rate adjustment settings.
